replies.author: @tobydelk2922
replies.text:
It's a hard truth, but you're far better off splurging a bit at a top-notch Japanese hibachi grill, sushi spot, or Korean BBQ joint—one where the chefs actually know their craft. The extra cost beats shelling out for the same bland slop at inflated prices, especially from McDonald's. I'll be straight with you: I've never been a fan, and it's not just because Ray Kroc could be a real sleaze. No, it's the whole operation—engineered to hook the thoughtless masses with addictive, empty calories that do more harm than good.

Burger King's always edged it out for me in taste and (relative) healthiness, and don't get me started on Captain D's or the other underdogs that punch above their weight. They're not the corporate giants, sure, but that's the point. Most folks would rather invest in real quality now and then than settle for mass-produced junk that's so preserved, a McDonald's burger could molder in a rainy cemetery for weeks without a hint of decay. Your wallet, your waistline, your call—but life's too short for that.

@superbrainz2357 Propably a main reason why the first movie worked and the second not so much.

The first at least focused on the adults and the conflict. Making it a meaningful story but with actual catching action scenes that also had real stakes. 

The alst duel was pretty good with the colonel in the suit going hand to hand. 

The second was leaning heavy on the spiritual aspect, cut out the focus of the main character and gave most screentime to the children. Wich works as a inturduction scene, or here and there. But taking the focus off of the characters people actually care about and of course what made the first one so great?

With the colonel now in a similar body as his enemies plus his badassery and knowledge you had the tools to create something truly great. And instead they fumbled it.

replies.text:
If the Avatar movies had no heart or soul, they wouldn’t have been popular successes at all. It’s like Spielberg said about Jurassic Park years after its release: it’s about a guy who doesn’t want kids, and it wouldn’t have been a hit if it had just been dino spectacle. 

As for Avatar, its story is archetypal and a frame for Cameron to hang his more idiosyncratic sci-fi ideas onto, but being archetypal doesn’t make it vapid.

replies.author: @SchrodingersTransCat
replies.text: Random retro game rec: Anyone keen to see what good gameplay looks like should check out Wild Guns, which came out in 1994 for the Super Nintendo. Made by five people in five months between other projects. It's one of those 'shooting gallery' games that you sometimes saw in arcades back in the day.  Has no interest in being realistic--you're fighting sci-fi cowboy robots. All it cares about is being fun. You can finish it in half an hour if you know what you're doing, but you'll have the time of your life doing so.

replies.author: @mattyboyanderson
replies.text:
Also, if you notice, even people who didn't like BTiLC still speak highly of it. 
It's unfair to compare any other director to John Carpenter though. No one can match the ongoing endurance of his "nothing but net" period. Imagine living in 2025 and having "They Live", "The Thing" and "Halloween" on your resume; the youngest film is almost four decades old, and none of them have been even close to surpassed, let alone equaled. Meanwhile Avatar will continue to resemble "Myst" on a late-1990's PC. Great for demo-ing HDTVs at Costco though, I'm sure.

replies.author: @Simon-xc5oy
replies.text: @mattyboyanderson I have never been able to grasp how Carpenter went from making things like Halloween, Escape from New York, The Thing, They Live and so on. To utter crap like Ghosts of Mars. I rewatched that last week, as I have not seen it in years. I was hoping it would have got better with time and I might be more forgiving of it. But its utter and total trash. Every single thing about it is bad. It looks cheap, its story is a zombie clone rip off and the acting is terrible along with every stereo type character you can think of. There is not a single thing in it that is tension, scary or unique and you are sitting there thinking all the time, that its so predictable. And you hope as its Carpenter, something different will take place or there will be some atmosphere and there is non at all, and if you can predict it, then that is exactly what happens. The first time I saw Halloween, I was an early teen in the mid 80s and I was utterly petrified, the music, the atmosphere, the build up, tension, locations, camera angles, sound effects, cast etc it was utterly perfect, all of it. And chilling. Ghosts of Mars is one of the worst things I have ever seen.

replies.author: @AltruisticMystic
replies.text: @Ricardo-88-14 It's okay to have an opinion and to not like some movie. But to openly talk about it publicly without realising why it is so amazing, you will get a shitstorm and you deserve it because you do not appreciate how original it is. Do you understand it is intentionally corny? Do you get the main ''thing'' about this movie? It's the first movie where the protagonist is actually the sidekick. Not only does it parody certain things, the visual effects are incredibly fun, the dialogue is on point for the theme of the movie, the timing of the jokes is impeccable, the movie deserves a cult following. Do you understand that Jack Burton is the stereotype macho man action hero, but at the same time, he is clueless and kinda lucky? And that his ''asian sidekick'' is actually the one that saves everyone? The main character basically has no idea what is going on but he is an American macho. He is respected for his bravery, but the dude is completely out of place. When you look at the DVD extra commentary, both the director and Kurt Russell are dying from laughter because of that character alone.

replies.author: @Simon-xc5oy
replies.text: Yes. That is EXACTLY what it is. When the last Water World one came out people were raving about the effects and how the detail on the blue smurfs etc was better and more improved than the 2009 original. When I watched it I just felt like I was watching a big long video game cut scene. It did nothing for me and left me cold. I was bored. And its just a beat you about the head eco message etc and how nasty and destructive mankind is. And it made me sad, thinking what Walt Disney did with animation in creating Snow White and doing something as a first that way. Compared to Avatar and how far things have moved on technology wise, but not in a good way that has any meaning. It reminds me of the old Amiga Demo scene. Some amazing work, pretty pictures and tunes and pushing the technology and showing off. Great. But they did nothing more than that. And this is all Avatar is, Cameron showing off tech.

author: @cpregal
text:
The Avatar films are Cameron’s Star Wars and Lord of the Rings. He is using the films to promote his technology stack of 3D rigs, underwater motion capture systems, virtual production tools, simulcam, etc. That is why he is setting the films in different biomes, to advance the motion capture and 3D tech in new settings. 

Cameron does this through CPG (Cameron Pace Group). The specific special effects work once the movie is filmed is handled by Weta FX. The actual filming tech that he is developing is what Cameron is interested in promoting and advancing. 

In light of that, the narrative itself simply has to be serviceable, supported by Cameron’s own unique brand of populist (not popular) filmmaking. 

The goal of Avatar is to promote filmmaking technology while simultaneously making Mount Everest sized gobs of money. In that light, Avatar is a resounding success. 

Interesting note: Weta FX is a New Zealand based company that was founded by Peter Jackson. The effects were used in Lord of the Rings and King Kong. In 2021, Jackson sold the VFX division for $1.62 billion. So there is a clear precedent in Jackson and Lucas for what Cameron is trying to accomplish.

replies.author: @cpregal
replies.text:
@KotifilosofiThe narrative for Avatar is absolutely perfect for its goal. It’s a sci-fi Dances With Wolves, a fish-out-of-water/white-man-goes native plot peppered with simple themes of populist class struggle and environmentalism. It is so simple that it can be understood by a 9 year old or a 90 year old.

If Cameron tampered with the plot too much it would go over some people’s heads. It might come across as offensive or political to others. The tone would be off and result in an uneven story that distracts from the spectacle of the CGI. 

There is a reason Cameron will be at the Hainan Film Festival in China for the premiere of Avatar 3 on December 19th. Avatar clears the censors in China with flying colors. Spider-Man was blocked because it had too much Statue of Liberty. Top Gun: Maverick was censored because of the Taiwanese flag on Tom Cruise’s bomber jacket. Avatar is considered harmless and apolitical. The same was true for Titanic. There is a reason three of the top five movies of all time are by James Cameron. A simple story with incredible special effects that can resonate across continents and cultures the world over needs to be delivered properly to become a blockbuster. Cameron is the absolute best at it. 

To quote Marshall McLuhen: “The medium is the message.” For Avatar, the CGI is the message. You ask too much of James Cameron. In light of his goals, he’s doing everything right with Avatar.

replies.author: @pizza2005
replies.text:
@malvoliosfI genuinely love the Avatar movies so much that I buy all the comics and spent the entire 13 year wait for the second movie checking the Wikipedia page looking for news on updates.  One of my greatest fears in life is that we will somehow not get Avatar 4 and 5 or something like that.  This is one of if not possibly THE masterpiece of cinema that will stand the test of time in ways most other things will not.  It’s just difficult to explain why that’s true.

I think more than anything that Avatar is not poorly written or boring or whatever else.  It’s simple.  I think a lot of people underestimate how important simplicity is for really connecting to the human condition.  This is a film franchise that can cross cultures in a way a lot of things might not be able to.  Hitting the kind of box office numbers these movies hit requires that you be popular in other countries like China as well, for instance.  You need to be accessible in a way that requires a sort of very basic connection to humanity.

Also, the world building is top notch.  So much effort was put into developing ecosystems, cultures, and other things.  It’s genuinely a fascinating world built with so much love and care.  It’s just that for many people what hooks them to writing and stories are character moments.  It’s the difference between reading a history textbook that documents the movement of troops vs one that documents the inner thoughts of Abe Lincoln.  The movie is also just popular enough that hating it for not “living up to the popularity” is the popular thing to do.  The fact is that no matter how many people insist that nobody talks about these movies, plenty of people talk about these movies, just almost always specifically to make fun of them or to complain about them for “not being good enough”.

I dunno.  One of my best friends and I have been going to rereleases and new releases for years and buying merch and comics and reading stuff and so forth.  I genuinely think that in 20 to 40 years there will be a lot of vindication for just how important and incredible these movies are in so many ways.  I really love them so much.

replies.author: @ericschumann4213
replies.text:
I strongly doubt that. The story itself is actually pretty vapid and the acting is ... lacking. In order for people to relate and develop an attachment to the characters, the movie needs at least two things. The characters have to have qualities or traits in common with the viewer that the viewer considers to be essential to their own person. The second thing that is needed is for the actor(s) to have enough charisma to instill an emotional bond with the viewer. Avatar failed on both counts. Very few people can identify with a traitorous soldier, no matter if they are justified or not, and certainly nobody was going to identify with the ignorant (not stupid) savages.

The second property is arguably more important, and was even more noticeably absent from Avatar. CGI, even today, is not good enough to overcome the uncanny valley. Even with human looking characters, something in the human mind rebels a little when presented with something that is very close to human, but the mind can still tell is fake. With an alien, its even worse. To make the viewer care about the characters, you have to trick them into a romantic attraction to one of the characters and have them believe the characters romantic partner in the movie is a stand-in for themselves. With the alien being a hard sell as romantic prospect, that puts a lot of pressure on the other leading character. Sam Worthington (I had to look up the actors name in spite of the drinker mentioning it at least once) is a pretty lousy actor. in a movie full of CGI, he manages to come off as the fakest thing somehow. His best acting scenes were the ones where his body is in a coma. That's a super bad setup to base your movie from.

If your movie doesn't have any character appeal, you can just dispense with the whole relationship thing, but then your movie better have deep thought provoking ideas. Avatar gives us very little outside of the two main characters, and as someone else mentioned, its basically just a rip-off of Dances with Wolves, which was itself far from an original story. There's no new point of view to consider, just the same tired, "Humans bad, nature good" sentiment that reminds me why I smile every time I remember Bruce Willis trying to hit Greenpeace protesters with golf balls in Armageddon. That one act gave Armageddon more story depth than all of Avatar.

I think the easiest fix for the 1-dimensional story of Avatar would have been to give the characters some real arc's. Have the characters grow in a meaningful way. Start Sam off as the Colonel or something so that he could meaningfully overcome some flaw in his own character. Maybe have the Quaritch character start as a super ambitious subordinate of Sullys and have Quaritch get promoted when Sully rebels. That would have been a lot more interesting. Point is there are a thousand ways to make the story better, but Cameron spent way too much time worrying about the visual effects and basically ignored the story (or lack thereof). That is why, although this may be his most profitable work, it will be remembered as the low point of his career.

replies.author: @Simon-xc5oy
replies.text:
GregKroll-u1x It did, but it was fun. Will Smith was good in it and so was Goldblum and Judd Hirsch and it was memorable for the effects shots of the White House getting blown up and New York. Those were iconic and well known and everywhere for a long time. And quotes like - " In the words of my generation....UP YOURS!!!" 
As Quade takes down the saucer, it was fun to see and laugh along with. A homage to all the 50s B movie Alien Invasion films was a great idea, and doing it with big budget style for a change.

replies.author: @Simon-xc5oy
replies.text: GregKroll-u1x Ok, no I did not like the version of Godzilla he did and it was indeed rubbish. And I did not like the sequel to ID4 either. It was terrible. And yes you are right the original is daft and mushy and so on but that was the point of it. It was supposed to be a sort of 50s Earth vs the Flying Saucers type cheesy movie. But done in the modern day (the then 90s) with modern big budget effects. Which is why they spent all the money on the effects not the stars etc. It was for the spectacle of it. And to see the USA get cities destroyed by Aliens. I happen to love the old 50s movies, stuff like Them, It came from another world, The Thing, Body Snatchers and so on and in the 90s we got this and stuff like Mars Attacks which was great fun to see in cinemas. Loved Mars Attacks it was utterly barmy star studded nonsense and another 50s style aliens film done with modern tech and cash. It was the start of the new disaster movie era with things like Dantes peak, Volcano and Daylight. These things came and go and you get a trend for a while like when they were making Cowboy films again with Tombstone, Unforgiven and others like it. At least they were trying and doing new films in old genres for the audience. Unlike today. But ID4 is about my only fave from that director / writing team, I just loved the idea of it and so did the audience as it was really successful and popular....

@vgramatski I wouldn't exactly know what you mean by that question.  I don't claim to remember anything about movies that I've never watched.

My point was, aside from the post-theatrical release reviews, how long did people talk about Way of Water?

Now, before _and_ after release, how much content did you see about Snow White?  The Little Mermaid?  Mulan?  The list goes on.  

I'm not making the argument that what Disney has been putting out has been _good_ , merely that it's been noteworthy for being terrible, and all the drama that's come with it ("weird, weird").

The Avatar movies might be "not terrible", by comparison to Disney, but they're hollow.  They're... the finest piece of plain white bread you'd ever eat.

Good point.

To be honest, when Cameron gave his glowing endorsement of that shitty Terminator flick that came out years ago, on which he was a producer - KNOWING what an absolute piece of shit it TOTALLY was, and therefore _completely selling out_ both his honor and his honesty - his word as an artist - it was the final straw, for me.

I knew then that he had finally - for SURE, and DEFINITIVELY - gone the way of Ridley Scott. I KNEW he was willing to flat-out lie, for whatever next multimillion-dollar paycheck/project (of his choosing) - without the mandatory minimum regard for its quality, which is absolutely necessary if I'm still going to take him at his word.

So his word means pretty much NOTHING to me, now, sadly - and I dare say he's done, as a legitimate storyteller. 

Unfortunately (😢), it's just impressive slop now (but hey - using "the greatest technology ever!")...

It's been since it's release since I've seen it, and I can honestly say I don't remember too much of it, so that probably says a lot about how much of it is not very memorable. replies.author: @Wurzelknecht
replies.text:
As someone who sneered at all the hype the first one got and merely found it a good-looking, decent rehash of Dances with Wolves in blue, I really liked the 2nd one and thought it was far superior to the first. It had a lot of heart. We have some new characters due to Jake and Neytiri's kids, as well as the new tribe they encounter, we get some family drama, some culture clashes, some mysteries, and what I found interesting in regards to the upcoming 3rd film: Neytiri becoming more and more hateful towards humans and the conflict that is bound to arise from that between her and Jake.

The one thing I didn't like about the movie was Jake himself, who hasn't gotten any wiser or smarter in the past 10 years, and is still the same old stupid and stubborn "I'm an American soldier, so I know what to do in any situation and nobody should question my judgment in situations that don't have anything to do with warfare"-character. He's kinda frustrating to watch throughout most of the movie as he is so sure he has all the answers, and any suggestions to the contrary by his wife and kids are shot down vehemently, like an incompetent officer pulling rank on his subordinates when they have a better idea than him to shut them up. It's consistent with how he was in the first movie, and I get that old habits are hard to drop, but I hoped that in the like 15-20 years that passed since the first movie, he would've progressed a little bit as a character in that regard.

replies.text: I enjoyed it, but the whole "Nature good. Tech bad." concept was a bit hamfisted here and there. One of the dumbest things was when Kiri starts having her seizures and the human doctor guys are trying to tell what's going on in her brain. "Oh don't hook her up to the Life Tree thing. That'll kill her!" I'm sorry but we're talking the biology of a completely NEURALLY integrated ecosystem and you want to cut her off from it? Yeah, you know what's going on in these giant blue cat people. Sure. At the same time potions and smoke are not healing. They're scrabbling in the dark. "Yeah we can cure alzheimers by murdering a definitely sentient non-humanoid. NO PROBLEM!" The minute you start hooking wires between the brains and literally any animal organically naturally every conception of our own moral justifications about FOOD go out the window. It's a mess.

​@revenge12212012, yeah, there're some unexpected parts like main hero who was a tactician and protector of the first tribe deserting to the other place to - as a movie plot implies - save that forest tribe from the constant war. But if you think about this plot twist even for a minute, you'd remember that Naavi were attacked in the first place not because of Jake Sully, but for economic reasons, and also main hero was the only decent strategist who'd defend local tribes.

So, when he departs from his tribe to the other place, he's actually abandoning the first for an extermination by the evil hoomies which makes the plot of the 2009 movie senseless. The second Avatar is not a good movie plotwise.

replies.author: @ryankramer
replies.text: @DanielDiaz-qo3wo For what it’s worth, Unobtainium is an actual term and not made up by Avatar. In science/ engineering it’s a real term to describe a hypothetical, impossible, rare, or very expensive material that would solve an engineering problem. It DOES sound goofy though. 😅

replies.author: @Norbert359
replies.text:
@electroglodytemojo
It's also a Vietnam War parable. How superior technology and weaponry can be beaten by more primitive people who actually have something to lose and fight for. Like Aliens was saying, "Don't underestimate the determination of a mother fighting for her child", these films are saying, "You can only push people so far before they fight back". In Trump's America that is more relevant now than it was when the first Avatar came out. 
I think that's the genius of the Avatar franchise. It's a template and you can take away from the movie whatever you bring to it. Rather than pure escapism, I think it's a more subtle way for people to process what is happening in the real world. Bring Israel and Palestine with you as you enter the cinema and you will see those parallels played out for you. Oppressors and oppressed. Exploiters and exploited. Corporate greed at the expense of the masses and the environment. You will see whatever is at the forefront of your mind when you watch these movies. I think people don't talk that much about these movies because it is a different experience for everyone that sees them.
But hey, those are just my thoughts on it and, after all l, who the hell am I...?🤷‍♂️

replies.text: It's really not that difficult. Avatar movies are the ultimate popcorn movies. Plenty of popcorn movies do very very well financially and nobody acts shocked. Avatar is simply the ultimate form of that kind of movie made by an obsessive man who won't stop until he's gotten what he wants. They are expertly crafted movies, well written, well shot, well directed and well-acted audio visual masterpieces. They are a roller coaster that everyone can get on, enjoy for a couple hours and then go on about their lives. That's why so many of that kind of movie does so well. I mean look at the Jurassic Park movies of the last decade plus. They're the same kind of movies only the Avatar movies are just done better across the board. The last Jurassic movie made just shy of 900m. If that movie can do that, why is it shocking that Avatar can make 2 billion? Those movies are just as culturally inert. The reality is that people will pay for top quality escapism.

replies.author: @mnomadvfx
replies.text:
I took years to finally watch the first, I wasn't impressed - especially due to having watched Dances with Wolves and seeing the immediate story similarities.

I had seen Ferngully before hand, but the story plagiarism didn't occur to me as I hadn't seen FG in a very long time.

That being said, plagiarised or not the story foundations of Avatar are good, the script is just a whole bag of meh and Sam Worthington really doesn't sell it for me.

If not for the spectacle of the CGI, especially the abundant facial mocap I wouldn't have found it interesting at all - but even then as others have said it looks more like a really beautiful tech demo for a new console than a compelling film.

The thing is that if you don't use a skill you will lose it, and James Cameron had so much money from Titanic that he literally just spent years playing about with deep sea subs and stuff rather than making films - and it shows in both the script and the general directorial quality of Avatar.

Then he had an equally long time to make Avatar 2 and what little skill he retained by the production of Avatar was clearly all but gone.

Honestly I think at this point if he just made a 'normal' live action film it would probably be worse than the majority of Michael Bay's works.

replies.author: @4Everlast
replies.text:
@kmm-k1e You are correct, sir. BUT, what's the deciding factory in your case? You refuse to watch heavy CGI'd movies or you just can NOT get into them?  None of us decide what we'll like before we see it, RIGHT?  I can actually objectively show why these movies are good, what most people have is subjective, not liking them. I can't fault them for it, they didn't choose to do so, but they can NOT say, a good movie ain't good. You see the problem?
I'm not a fan of CGI, haven't played a game in 10+ years.

But do we stumble on a cartoon, anime, clay-mation occasionally?
Am I being pulled out cause it's showing something like a car, but it doesn't feel realistic cause it's hand drawn?! My brain adjusts to the "realism" of the movie, no matter how realistic a movie is i NEVER forget it's a movie, so no, CGI doesn't bother me if the story can't be told without it and it's a good story, and Avatar is a good, layered story no matter how you spin it.

Would you watch it in an old school animated form tho? It would lend it self nicely tho.

Give it another shot in a few years.

replies.author: @kmm-k1e
replies.text:
@4Everlast > I can actually objectively show why these movies are good

Technically sure, no argument from me. Pretty much as good as this genre of production gets, but I find the style tiresome, visually busy, and overly saturated. CGI lets VFX producers do _whatever_ they want, with no barriers or constraints other than how many hours of animator and render cluster time they can pay for. 

I've never found it to be realistic. The cameras are doing impossible movements, there's often little sense of inertia or mass, and when superheroes start battling it looks like a candyflipper rave lightshow. 

 > Would you watch it in an old school animated form tho?

I'm a fan of traditional animation just like I'm a fan of traditional VFX, I wish the west still made high-budget traditional animation. We already have Avatar in traditional animation format though, it's just called Fern Gully.

replies.author: @potetnamnbaknamn895
replies.text: @4Everlast lmao, like people havent been saying that shit since the 90s.

replies.author: @4Everlast
replies.text:
@kmm-k1e Honestly, this is 100% a subjective problem. A question of taste. It's a SF movie ("visually busy" by default), spectacle larger then life (visually busy by default), taking place in an alien life-rich jungle! (visually busy...) It's a sensory overload by design, well for some of you. On top of that it's an action spectacle in an already overkill-ADHD having generation that's not only used too, but embraced a cluttered visual (and digital) space.

The movie is set on a foundation of visual wealth, an explosion of colors and it's proud of it, so I'm not sure what did you expect, want nor need, but it's clearly not for you by your own default sensibilities. The "YOU" part is key. That can't nor isn't hindering it's actual quality in any shape nor form tho.

The cameras doing impossible things, is a good way to describe a modern movie, and as someone studying film for the past 15 years, I can't really remember where in Avatar does a camera do such a thing? Cause I'm THE 1st person to be pulled out of the movie cause of it.
What you might mean is the camera, well the frame is always full to the brim, but that's by design, as you'd see that your self if you were standing there and a bit more then that, BUT that's a part of the experience as this movies are. At least in cinema.
There are a few dynamic zooms that are an editing choice but they are just another tool in the tool belt.
LOTR are a good example of camera going nuts.

Actually I'd argue these movies are one of the rare ones where giant CGI items actually feel like they are as massive as they are. The mechs are putting your sound system through a battle, the giant trucks and the three crashing is some of THE best massive scale CGI items ever to be put to screen! There's a notion the fkn sky is falling that's how large it looked and felt. In the 2nd one the train sequence and the last explosion set up as a distraction rattled the speakers, if you didn't feel that one I don't know wtf to say. Might be the sound at your cinema that's fucked? It happens often.

The battles feel like big creatures are colliding with big creatures, not sure what you mean, sound design is flawless, the rounds zooming by the camera is tremendous. I'm telling you, our minds have an way of feeling what's real and what's not, we never saw these creatures in real life nor the mech and it's difficult for us to relate, not to mention how ever great the CGI got it's still got work to do. The water and the skin in Avatar 2 are PHOTOREALISTIC. More to come.

Ahhh "a fan of traditional animation". There we go, that's the key here. You'd eat this up if it was in an actual old school hand drawn animated form, I wouldn't mind it either, but I'd take both version if I could. Animation, done right has a quality to it that's almost impossible to describe. As if you actually entered another dimension.


Do not start with the Fern Gully BS, you're better then that. Those are buzzwords for trolls who never saw the movie. You can compare ANY movie to another one, you can break down ANY movie in the common denominator kind'a way, Avatar's got way to much going for it to be compared to another thing based on 3 details.

replies.author: @kmm-k1e
replies.text: @4Everlast Yeah mate I said I don't get into CGI and most of it is just that I don't like the feel or aesthetics. Kubrick's 2001 is one of my favorite sci-fi movies and a lot of people find it mind-numbingly boring, but I find it extremely interesting especially visually, just as a thing to look at and take in. Favorite "CGI" movie would be Jurassic Park (1993), and the fact that the VFX still hold up almost perfectly is a testament to how skilled the staff were at using the primitive CGI workflows and techniques of the time, working within the limits of the media and coming up with creative workarounds to things that simply couldn't be done with the tech of the time.

replies.author: @4Everlast
@kmm-k1e
replies.text: @4Everlast Dune was fantastic as well. One of the few recent movies that really impressed me with its cinematography, set design, and mix of grounded CG and practical SFX (iirc they shot the Geidi Prime sequences in  near-infrared to give that stark and uncanny look, absolutely brilliant). Haven't seen Prometheus but the the clips I've seen do look good visually.

replies.author: @4Everlast
replies.text:
@kmm-k1e 100%  I could watch a 2 hour longer version, no problem. A super detail-rich world even tho it's visually "simple". A wonderful adaptation that I hope even the fans of the book get all they wished for, soon.
The infrared trick was soooo effective, sooo perfectly utilized, a joy to watch.

If you saw the 1st 4 Aliens, and 2 Predators you got to watch Prometheus. It's the most beautiful mess you've ever seen, it has the potential to be yet another top 5 SF horror from Ridley, yet they dropped the ball on every and any way imaginable.
Shame, cause it had ideas strong enough to stand on it's own 2 legs, but that's what you get when you do deals with a studio and without a script. He did it with Gladiator and thinks he can do it again, at the expense of a destruction of several of his universes. Quite a thing to behold.

The film actually tracks disturbingly close to Cameron's _actual_ life, except without the Happy Ending. Coincidentally, the very next film revolves around the 'Heart' of a MACHINE... and then all subsequent films HAVE no Heart. 

There is a deep bitterness and lack of hope that runs through the undercurrent of all of his later creations, regardless of what 'good' appears on the surface. 

'Aliens 3', which burns the heart right out of 'Aliens' from the start, would fit perfectly with Cameron's vibe post 'Abyss/T2'.

These movies are just 'Lawrence of Arabia' in space, with the 'noble savage' trope and romanticized notion of Native American mysticism and superstition cranked up to 11.

I find the plot incredibly dull and unoriginal, because I'm not a naive child and while I have a deep appreciation for nature, I don't think that nature is magic and neither are any native peoples.

Dune was also 'Lawrence of Arabia' in space, but it had a plot and characters that went a lot deeper than that.

author: @snufkinjay3774
text:
I just wanted to remind everyone that the first Avatar drew crowds to the cinemas not because it had an extraordinary story, but mainly because of the innovative technology used in its production, which was focused on delivering the film in 3D.

Back in 2009, watching a full-length fabular movie in 3D was a novelty and felt like the next big step in how we experience cinema. That, together with a simple, family-friendly plot, made everyone want to see it. We all know what happened with the 3D trend afterward.

I completely agree that the second part came out too late and no longer made much of an impression on anyone. :)

replies.author: @snufkinjay3774
replies.text:
Just to be clear, I’m not trying to defend the movie as a whole. :) I simply wanted to point out that one of the biggest reasons for its popularity was the fact that most of its scenes were shot specifically with 3D viewing in mind. That aspect alone played a huge role in its marketing. 

For some people it wasn’t entirely new — I still remember 3D VHS tapes you’d watch on a TV using those paper glasses, ^^  and there were also special cinemas showing 3D documentaries.  Even so, Avatar was one of the first feature-length films to break into the mainstream and kick off a new trend — one that, to put it gently, never really took off.


Still, it was a kind of phenomenon, and for certain generations, despite all its obvious flaws, it might remain an experience they truly remember. ;)

replies.author: @dycedargselderbrother5353
replies.text: @snufkinjay3774 Yeah, before Avatar, 3D was an afterthought in terms of production design. Avatar had much better depth than any previous movie, at least the ones I had seen.

replies.author: @snufkinjay3774
replies.text:
@dycedargselderbrother5353  You’re completely right.  : )

After Avatar, nearly every movie in cinemas suddenly had a 3D tag and tried to cash in on the same success. The issue was that most of them were converted into 3D only after the films were already completed, rather than being shot with 3D in mind from the start — and that made a huge difference in how they were experienced.  In the end, it completely killed the whole 3D trend, whatever one might think of it. :)

replies.author: @williamwinham4421
replies.text:
@manuelper Maybe for you, but the movie actually started to gain popularity not just during its time in theaters, but after it left the theaters.  A lot of people listened to "the professional critics" and never went to see it in the theater.  A lot of people saw Alita for the first time while flying in a plane.  It's understandable...you're on a 3-4 hour flight, you're bored, and you look up what the in-flight movie is.  Many of these individuals made the same comment on social media afterward, and the comment was basically "Why didn't I go see this in the theater?!"  
The movie you saw was meant to be an introduction to the character of Alita, more was supposed to follow.  The Alita movie was supposed to be the first part of a trilogy, so no, you never saw just how amazing the character truly was.  
Even though Alita Battle Angel was just an introduction to the character, the character had more personality and heart than in any of the characters of Avatar from the first Avatar to the latest.  
Rosa Salazar did a great job bringing Alita to life, and Christoph Waltz did his usual amazing performance.  
I saw Avatar in the theaters twice.  I saw Alita in the theaters three times.  You know what I saw at every Alita showing that I never saw at the Avatar showings?  People crying during the movie because of the depth of the emotions between the characters.

replies.author: @ericschumann4213
replies.text: What are you talking about? Avatar was one of the most super-hyped movies of all time. The marketing machine was in full swing for the original. They spent a couple hundred million marketing that movie, and most of it went to unconventional (for the time) marketing which is why it actually worked. They paid an army of posters to write comments mentioning the movie on sites like Reddit and Slashdot. You could always tell it was a shill because it was always from a band new account that signed up, posted one comment about the upcoming Avatar movie and then never logged in again, or sometimes a pair of accounts would go back and forth for a few posts and then disappear. James Cameron didn't invent AstroTurfing, but he knew who to hire to get the job done well.

WHAT???????????????????? I get its popular and its a musical etc...but paying to see it in cinemas? Its on tv every week and twice or three times at Christmas! It drives me up the wall, how many times its repeated. My ex wife used to love it. One bank holiday weekend at her parents, she sat watching that all afternoon with her mum and dad etc...while I was sanding woodwork down and painting the kitchen for them. I would rather do that and sit through Sound of Music. 
Mind you I get it, I understand why and where you are coming from. As this evening I just watched Chitty Chitty Bang Bang in my home cinema. I have a projector etc and full speaker kit and I had not watched it for years and I wanted to see something charming and uplifting. Something that took me back to when I was about 7 years old and first saw it on tv. I got really weepy a few times watching it. So many of the cast are no longer with us. The England its set in no longer exists. The songs were lovely and the car is magnificent. Dick Van Dyke is brilliant, so funny and they just dont make things like this anymore. The score is incredible. I still love Posh Posh travelling life, Grow the Roses and Me Old Bamboo they are brilliant...Its a timeless film from a more innocent age, no agenda, no woke rubbish and positive male and female role models looking after the children....

replies.author: @saribeepo.o5111
replies.text:
Here's the thing though, they only care about the world because of how good it looks. That's really all it has going for it. What did you see that was unique about this world?  The benevolent give mind? Seen it. Every animal they have is based on something we've seen before. That's not the dog on them, it's hard to come up with a whole ecosystem, but the fact is the idea of bioluminescent force is not unique. Cat like predator, dog like predators, raptor like predators that look a little bit more like dragons none of this is particularly special.  Aside from their 'god' which mirrors a basic concept of mother Earth aside from the supernatural stuff it's just 'trial life' as we stereotype it.

In fact, the world building is so flimsy in the first film, that you have it retconned in the next films. We're using the convenient, well the world is big and we just didn't see these people before nonsense.  We saw Jake enlist a coastal people in the first film, yet all of a sudden we have a tribe that doesn't care about about their god.  We were sold on a people that were brought up to believe that everything is done for the balance of the world, so they don't expect they're god to intervene for them, but this new film is a clan that's mad because a natural disaster happened and their god didn't do anything about it.

replies.author: @rasmusrossnylandsted9205
replies.text:
@saribeepo.o5111 I heavily disagree brother. What is inherently unique about anything in media? What does anything do that we have not already seen a million times before? For world-building every good idea will be a re-combination of other ideas, or risk becoming a random confusing mess which we have no way of relating to with our human experience.
The greatest strength of avatar’s world is exactly its relatability. It keeps this tether while exaggerating its features and making them as “alien” as they possibly can be. This is a exactly what makes it such a marvelous piece of escapism - and far from a thoughtless one.

From the biological consistency of its plants and animals, to the intrinsic design of the na’vi culture, everything has been crafted with such care and effort: The Navi tongue is nearly a complete language. The first draft of music had them almost create a unique scale system based on four-fingered practitioners. Even the floating rocks are explained in terms of superconducting minerals suspended in magnetic fields. 
All these things are there; yet the world feels no pressure to explain itself to you nor show everything there could be. This does, in my opinion, only make the world feel MORE alive, as it gets expanded throughout the movies (especially as the more complex sides of the na’vi are shown).

If nothing else, we are in a setting, where the god of the indigenous is literally confirmed to be true, in conjunction with the ecology being a supercomputer that the indigenous can tap into. However downplayed, this surely is as novel a sci-fi concept as anything!
Sure, the films may struggle with other aspects, and their world-building isn’t for everyone. But still, many DO appreciate it, and it saddens me to see it being disregarded so superficially.

replies.author: @Raefire6
replies.text:
This is why I think the first movie worked better than the second one did. It didn't pretend the RDA represented the whole of humanity and even showed both humans being reasonable and compassionate and Na'vi being unreasonable and prejudice. It's not about species, it's about choice. It's not great, but it's there. The second movie backtracks so hard on this, even in the High Ground comic set during it. Every human is evil no matter their choices, even though there is a lot more than last time and apparently they're planning on colonizating Pandora but nothing they do seems to serve that goal. And every Na'vi is good and pure no matter their choices, even when they attempt to murder innocents, specifically Neytiri trying to kill Spider for no reason besides being human and having Quaritch as a biological dad. 

I don't expect the third movie to do better at this, but having humanity be the cartoonish villains without any real plans, personality or variety is not a selling point.

author: @dinorocker8647
text: This is what's wrong with Cameron's films today. The early films he did were incredible with the fact aside from the stories bein' incredible. They were stories about the human spirit overcoming insurmountable odds and triumphing. Shit, in the original Terminator, a little over halfway through the film you've already gone through an unbelievable rollercoaster ride with Reese and Sarah barely escaping death multiple times already. While in their hotel room, Sarah says "It'll never be over, will it?". When you hear that you think "Is this film gonna end, did I just buy a ticket to a never ending nightmare I can't escape from?" That was the sheer power of the first big James Cameron directorial effort. Which is mind blowing considering the film's barely past 90 minutes in length. It was about believing humans can triumph and band together. Now his films are the complete opposite, instead of films about the strength of the human spirit and hope for the future. They're basically propaganda films condemning us, saying "Humans destroy everything, the environment, each other. We're basically all a bunch of worthless animals." Which is why I don't bother with his films anymore

@suttagi And yet in spite of that, we have hopeful people watching and measuring the Ozone layer which is strangely self-repairing now that we're making efforts to revolutionize the way we do industry. 

We also have people tossing foundational efforts into the coral reef to help it rebuild. In fact two of them over the last 40 years have made serious recoveries and one artificial one now matches the natural ones in beauty and considered a great success at rehabilitation. 

Is it true that humans can be destructively greedy and detrimental to the environment? 

Yes.

But it's also true that we recognize our impact and that there are those of us out there who seek to rehabilitate the environment knowing that much like the great cathedrals of old. They won't be around to appreciate it. But their grandchildren will. And they can rest easy knowing they left something behind.

replies.author: @paradox2210
replies.text:
⁠​⁠@suttagi You don’t understand scientific method. It's clear just by the way you’re going about writing your comment as if random approved scientific papers are the gospel. 
Don't you *dare* say that that something which is only theoretical science, such as the current theory of climate change, is absolutely 'the truth' just because it’s currently considered as the 'most likely' reality. 
For your information, we are NOT the 'leading cause' for those environmental effects happening in the first place, as they would, in accordance with the current theory, eventually happen anyway regardless of human interaction - we are one of many causes of environmental change in the world (albeit a big factor), with the world itself being the primary factor. Yes, the planet changes its environments anyway - we know this because we can find marine fossils in mountainous rocks and fossils of animals such as hypsilophodonts (which would likely only survive in warm environments) in Antarctica. Yet the world never ended because Antarctica's ice didn’t exist, did it? In fact, there is more evidence to suggest that the world having ice caps is in and of itself unusual. Of course, this evidence can change, and new theory can emerge - that is the essence of science.
Science is meant to be questioned _by design_ - don’t pretend everything currently 'proven' by science is the truth! 😒

also i literally gave you a good source in my previous comment, yet you clearly ignored it. but sure, here's some more that corrolate or directly link human activity to thousands upon thousands of species deaths from today to all the way back to the ice age and that humans are the leading cause:

stanford report - "Study finds human-driven mass extinction is eliminating entire branches of the tree of life"

"Based on the historic genus extinction rate among mammals – estimated for the authors by Anthony Barnosky, professor emeritus of integrative biology at UC Berkeley – the current rate of vertebrate genus extinction exceeds that of the last million years by 35 times. This means that, without human influence, Earth would likely have lost only two genera during that time. In five centuries, human actions have triggered a surge of genus extinctions that would otherwise have taken 18,000 years to accumulate – what the paper calls a “biological annihilation.”"

discover wildlife - ""99 per cent of the species that have ever lived have gone extinct""

"Thanks to human-driven habitat destruction, pollution, introduced species, disease, hunting, climate change and various other influences, the rate and pattern of species decline and loss we are seeing now is as much as 10,000 times higher than you’d typically expect, which, based on evidence from the fossil record, usually sits at about one species per million per year. Our current rate of loss rivals some of the worst from previous mass extinctions and will be the first mass extinction event in the past 3.5 billion years that has been caused by a single species."

naturalhistory - "Extinction of Plants and Animals"

"Regardless, scientists agree that today’s extinction rate is hundreds, or even thousands, of times higher than the natural baseline rate. Judging from the fossil record, the baseline extinction rate is about one species per every one million species per year. Scientists are racing to catalogue the biodiversity on Earth, working against the clock as extinctions continue to occur."

"At the end of the last ice age, 10,000 years ago, many North American animals went extinct, including mammoths, mastodons, and glyptodonts. While climate changes were a factor, paleontologists have evidence that overhunting by humans was also to blame"

"Starting in the 1800s, industrialization drove up extinction rates and has continued to do so. For example, Chinese river dolphins, foothill yellow-legged frogs, and sockeye salmon are among the many species currently endangered by water pollution, dams, and other industrial pressures on rivers. Smithsonian Anthropologist Dr. Torben Rick leads an effort to understand how human activities affect biodiversity by studying interactions between humans and other species in the Channel Islands from ancient to modern times."

biodiversity council - "New study finds Australia's extinction tally is far greater than previously recognised"

"A new study estimates that between 1-3 species of insects and other native invertebrates like worms, snails and spiders, are becoming extinct in Australia every week, and about 9000 have become extinct since European arrival in 1788."

"“Thousands of invertebrate species remain at high risk of extinction,” said Dr Marsh, “But we don’t have to accept their losses as inevitable. There is a lot we can do to prevent extinctions, including by protecting important habitats and reducing threats, such as pesticide use."

"“Many invertebrate species have already lost a huge amount of habitat and ongoing habitat loss remains a major threat. 

“For example the golden sun moth occurs in south-eastern Australian grasslands. Even though little of the once vast grasslands remain they are still at high risk of being destroyed by developments."

@suttagi Because your cries of 'we should have always known better' speak to an element of omnipotence that humans don't possess. 

You need to drop that line of thinking. 

This is the first time you've actually admitted to people taking the time to learn stuff.

And the honest truth? 

Conservation efforts are a relatively new thing in the grand scheme of human history. Out of 6500 years we as a species have existed, Natural Conservation has only been a major profession for what? 100 years? It's only been 150 since the Industrial Revolution which brought on most of the issues you're lamenting about. 

Most natural disaster responses like oil spills and the means to handle those is barely 50 years at most. Even the Japanese who spent a good chunk of research on cleaning up radiological spills only recently deployed tech that could handle that in the wake of an earthquake damaging one of their plants less then 10 years ago. 

When you start actually looking at the numbers, there's actual hope to be found there and we're only just getting started.

replies.author: @pizza2005
replies.text: @dinorocker8647The first movie has no themes about saving humanity, the plot is quite literally that a corporation is paying mercenary soldiers to kill natives of a planet in order to mine resources to take back to Earth.  The second movie then talks about how the corporations have decided that Earth is a lost cause not worth saving and that it’s time to colonize Pandora for humans instead.  The point of the movie is that greed is evil, and that we should value nature.  In the 4th or 5th movie we will be returning to Earth and we’ll see more of what’s happened as a result of corporate greed, and from what I understand we will get more of the message about the fight to save Earth as well.  The people who believe that these movies are anti-human ignore that at least ⅓ to ½ of the “good guys” in these movies are actual literal humans, and the bad guys are meant to be the traitors to our species, not Jake, for deciding that killing sentient beings and exploiting nature is fine just for the sake of someone’s wallet.

author: @jeremyvanneman8112
text:
Ironically I'd argue they had the workings of a great story with Sam not having the use of his legs, and taking the place of his identical twin brother. 

They could have given him interesting glimpses into what the brother was discovering as a scientist for the mission and that he was killed because he was fighting against "the company" and that could have been worked into the way the avatar bodies are grown. They could have made a point to highlight how massive of a lasting impact it would have to go from confined to a wheelchair to having a super strong body with full functionality and hair that can merge brains (beyond a single shot that was definitely coordinated by Tarantino).

They could have done something with a lot of what they vaguely introduced. But instead those were even more tick boxes that were immediately dropped.

replies.author: @booradley5150
replies.text:
​​@jurgen951 What does Jurrasic Park have to do with anything?  The wave you speak of is metaphorical, not governed by immutable physical laws. The Avatar wave you speak of has generated a healthy $5 billion. That does not sound like a dead wave to me. However if it is, then it's a safe bet that every working and aspiring filmmaker dreams of such failure. 😂 

And when somebody explains what they mean by "cultural impact," we can have that discussion. But I think that's just meaningless jargon used by cynical edgelords who are dead inside, trying to come to grips with their envy and the knowledge that their last brown snake poking out from the water in the toilet bowl is the upward limit of their "cultural impact."

Jurassic movies aren't entitled to success because the first one was successful. Just because a franchise starts successfully it is no guarantee of continued success. See the Matrix for proof of that.

Oh, and Jake is NOT the hero, he's the villain.

author: @mrmastaofdesasta6994
text: I would have to disagree that the Avatar movies have no soul. It's just that the soul is not in the characters, but in Pandora itself. The story and people are secondary to the movies, which is why they are barely remembered. What the movies try to do is to show a beautiful alien world that evokes a feeling of longing in people. Which is an actually observed phenomenon btw. After the first movie came out, and also after the second, a large part of people who watched the movie reported feeling a strange homesickness for Pandora. But "homesickness" fades quickly, and so we only remember the spectacular sights from time to time when we think back to the movie, and everything else fades into background noise.

Why even bother have the movies take place on Pandora if these supposed advanced aliens act like stupid, petty humans? No wonder Sully integrated so quickly and easily into their culture, Pandora is just Earth! The first film was great, but the second really jumped the shark and the next 4 films are supposed to be just like it? Sully going around changing the minds of every racist tribe on Pandora while fighting the same army guy from the first film like a returning Saturday morning cartoon antagonist? Sign me out.

author: @tjjordan4207
text:
I could be very wrong on this but I think what helped the 2nd film make $2 billion was simply that it took 13 years to finally be made after hearing about it for so long.  Add on that it has beautiful visuals and taking place out in the ocean, which James Cameron is very much known for, and also came out during a time period when we were begging for a cinematic experience of some kind during the winter season when we're not going outside, it was able to make $2 billion again like it needed to.

But for the 3rd movie, I don't know if Cameron can pull off another $2 billion dollar hit again.  For one, the gap this time is only 3 years and now that we've seen what Cameron had to offer with the last movie, it doesn't make one interested in going to see what the new one is going to be like.  Second, what else can Cameron do to have spectacle visuals that we haven't already seen before?  And third, everything is far more expensive now than they were back in 2022.

But the biggest thing that comes to mind is that the 2nd movie was made over the course of a decade, so the Cameron we had making that movie from then is not the same Cameron who is making the sequels that will come after.  I don't think the masculinity that was portrayed in the first two films is going to be in these next ones.  It could very well become a girl-bossy flick, possibly to MCU levels.

But who knows, maybe I'm wrong and it'll make $2 billion again.  We'll have to wait and see.

I mean yeah, reshoots could happen, but considering how flawless the CG looks, I don't think they'll be rejigging shots a week before release. Unlike some other recent effects-heavy productions...

replies.author: @bajeebus-j2p
replies.text:
meh, seemed like a classic storyline told another way, and has many cool plots and beautiful worldbuilding

similar to many other stories easily enjoyed and offer thought-provoking ideas to chew on

it stuck with me tbh, i enjoyed watching it in theatres, larger-than-life vibes, and made me think about being a lifeform, being a human

also i remember thinking about like alien life, simulation stuff, connections with people, mythological stuff amongst living beings (humans can experience mythological things, and also evolved to do science, or, trying to uncover reality with as much accuracy as possible) - also it inspired ideas about war and combat, mortality and violence, and related to the actions of individuals and their minds and behavior patterns

also i like fluid and athletic body movements - i also enjoy this in things like disney's tarzan for example, and many other things - humans are of course animals and loving to move fluidly is common 

in short, yeah im not really seeing the whole "its bad" angle - its a story with many enriching features - which is extremely common - humans are quite good at making stories with enriching features, especially during this time

author: @natethegoodenough
text: As soon as the first movie turned out to be little more than a flashy mashup of "Dances With Wolves," "Pocahontas", and "Fern Gully," I didn't expect anything new beyond the admittedly stunning visuals. Feel free to see it in the theaters once, and then forget about it.

author: @magnanimousj
text: I can't explain why, but creators work best under limitations. You give them unlimited budget and technology to put anything their imagination can conceive onto the screen, and it's hollow and forgettable. I don't think it can be fully explained by an aging director who has been a legend for decades losing his passion, either. Best I can come up with is that the problem solving aspect of having to work around budgets and limitations of tech, etc, activate some part of the creative process that's necessary for greatness.

replies.author: @Stevarooni
replies.text: Technical and budget limitations mean that you _have_ to focus on the story and acting far more than the set and special effects.  The director will do everything he can within his location, costume, and SFX budget, which requires creativity, but he won't _be able_ do too much in those directions.😊

replies.author: @realRandomYT05
replies.text: Fast food should only be consumed once or twice a week at most. Preferably once or twice a month. Best if you never do it at all and just try your best to recreate the menu items at home. At least I know a home made burger and French fries hand cut and pan fried are better for me than the same tiny portion I'm getting from McDonald's with all their added chemicals and weird pink pastes. And when it comes to the soda, we also make it at home too because we have a water carbonator and cola syrups, which mind you have faaaar less sugar than a traditional coca cola. Although I personally add extra syrup for a sweeter drink. Still though, I can make burgers, fries, and soda, at home, its less calories, and I'm actually more satisfied than if I had gone to McDonald's.

replies.author: @Gwyn.Lord.of.Cinder4185
replies.text:
_Spare us your pity, alien. You gush about your connection with nature, your primal wisdom, but what has it brought you?_

_Where are your marvels of engineering? Your voyages of discovery? Your great insight into the nature of the universe? Even at our basest, when we dressed as you do, dwelt as you do, hunted as you do, lived as you do, we did more than merely survive. We built wonders. We made great journeys. We forged epics. You have not._

_You speak so proudly of the plugs dangling from your skulls, little realizing that they are but strings and you puppets. What little you have accomplished you attribute to your goddess, who is nothing but the voices of your dead echoing for all eternity. She moors you to the past, serving as a leash that keeps you little better than apes, sad parodies of civilization that lack that special spark to become something more._

_We have come to your world in search of resources. Whether your actions drive us back or we take what we want and move on, the outcome is the same. We will depart from your wretched planet, leaving you behind. And in a thousand years, you will not have changed from this contact with another world. You will remain in your trees, hunting your prey, communing with goddess, until your sun burns out and your world dies._

_And above your tomb, the stars will belong to us._

author: @Snarknado5
text: Avatar 1 came at a time when lots of people genuinely worried about the planet, and its environmentalist message stuck. Avatar 2 came at a time when most people associated climate advocacy with smug assholes throwing soup at paintings and impeding road traffic. We're in a post-idealistic era, and all that's left to Avatar is visual bombast- with which we've been over-fed.

replies.text: @Grenade123_2 It was about as much anti-whaling film as Titanic was anti-iceberg film.

replies.author: @Grenade123_2
replies.text: ​@darhaall The entire core message was "look at how evil humanity is this time for wanting to kill these majestic creatures for their own selfish reasons." They added in Sully's story line to throw in a little anti-Vietnam/Afghanistan war propaganda (because we aren't outdated and paint by numbers enough) and vaguely link the two movies together. He exists in that story just to be an excuse for the "peace-loving" groups (the water tribe that has been avoiding starting conflict despite clearly want it) and the whales (the one exiled one already is a resistance fighter before everyone else got the idea) to wage war. They don't even kill off the main bad guy, he is literally left alive because he didn't do any whaling (the whaler dude did die, to a whale). They claim victory when the whaling boat is destroyed. It is basically a "what if the Sea Shepard boat had actual firepower" instead of using Molotov's and stink bombs.

replies.author: @SkipTwo
replies.text: @TimHammond-o4qspot on sir…

replies.author: @darhaall
replies.text: @Grenade123_2 LOL, thats nonsense - entire Avatar saga is story of Sully. And his arch nemesis, thats why is left alive - to be part of finale. That lone whale has a nice story, but its just a subplot, movie could just as well exist without it.

replies.author: @Grenade123_2
replies.text: ​@darhaall my guy, the message of each movie is clear. The plot is just a vessel. First one was anti-mining/deforestation with anti-colonialism thrown in for spice. Generic paint by numbers Pocahontas/Dances With Wolves story line is just for mass appeal.

replies.author: @darhaall
replies.text: @Grenade123_2 Whales are taking up about 25 minutes out of 3+ hour movie, but sure, its all about the whales :D

replies.author: @Grenade123_2
replies.text:
​@darhaall 25 minutes? They made the whaling ship the entire focus of the second half of the movie. At least a third of Act 2 is dedicated to the whales and then all of Act 3 is taking down the evil whaling ship. Literally could have been any other kind of ship. I mean, it could have been a straight up military assault ship. They had a freaking gunship in the first movie, not like it isn't RDA standard. They also made whales the key deciding factor in the final battle and made them the focus of why "humans are bad" of this movie. They didn't need to do any of that. The strip-mining from the first movie was reason enough. There is no reason to assume they still don't need unobtanium. It seems pretty clear that the conflict forcing jake and them to leave started up again entirely because of their mining and colonization efforts. Humans are already the villains. Yet they decided to make them even more comically evil by throwing in whaling mixed with wasteful hunting practices. Why would they do that if they didn't want the theme, or at least the variant of the main "humans cause ecological destruction" theme, to be about whaling? 

By your logic the first movie didn't have an anti-mining or deforestation theme because only half of the first act has anything to do with mining, and  it is only referenced as the reason for destroying home tree in the second act.

replies.author: @darhaall
replies.text:
@Grenade123_2 I literally counted it, it is 25 minutes - last act is on whaling ship, but because of kidnapped children and hunt for Sully. Whaling is just something to piss off natives so they reveal themselves. 

Mining in first movie had much bigger focus, but that to was just a subplot - instead of mining it could be something else to paraphrase colonisation of america and taking land from indians - main theme was always Sully, his relationship with natives and with nature in general. And with all-encompassing planetary wide inteligence, alien personification of that nature, like gaia theory on earth.

replies.author: @collincutler4992
replies.text:
​​@theguyfromsaturnyou cannot compare those 2 movies to anything in modern day. Most of society could afford to go to a movie, much less have the media reach to report on one.
Take Infinity Wars for example. Subtract the merchandising opportunities and you still have a movie that nearly all other Marvel movies are compared to these days.
EVERYONE was talking about it, going to see it multiple times. Idk how many videos I've seen dissecting the gray morality of Thanos's decisions and whether he was justified in his actions.
To this day I still se memes about "Thanos was right"
That movie made people talk and think.
Cultural impacts are movies that define a generation..... Terminator, Jurassic Park, Infinity War, etc...
